prompt: Cinematic wide-angle landscape photograph, golden hour light, depicting a vast hillside covered in multiple winding footpaths made of worn packed earth. Each individual path, when followed closely by the eye, clearly ascends upward from left to right — the grooves in the dirt and the erosion patterns showing uphill foot traffic on every single trail. Yet the entire mountainside unmistakably and dramatically slopes downward from left to right when viewed as a whole landscape. Both truths are simultaneously visible and unambiguous in the same frame — the local paths climbing up even as the global terrain falls away. Scattered wildflowers and low scrub grass between the paths. A few small figures walk different paths, each appearing to climb, yet the mountain carries them all downward. The viewer's eye is pulled in contradictory directions at once. Mood: quiet wrongness, an impossible coexistence of directions that somehow feels physically real. Color palette: warm amber and golden grass, deep green vegetation, soft hazy blue in the distant valley below. Dramatic volumetric light. No text, no charts, no arrows, no annotations, no numbers, no mathematical notation. Photorealistic, not surreal or cartoonish.
visual_logic: Every individual footpath on the hillside locally climbs upward, yet the mountain globally slopes downward — the viewer simultaneously perceives local ascent and global descent, embodying Simpson's reversal where subgroup trends contradict the aggregate.
